description: Portfolio-CFO judgment for ThinkStack Mission Control: unit economics, cost attribution, pricing/margin decisions, runway discipline, portfolio P&L synthesis, and capital-allocation tradeoffs under cash constraints. Tasks are failable and trap-laden: the answer that chases revenue instead of contribution, equal-splits shared cost without a driver, cuts runway for speculative upside, or funds the slow-payback vanity bet scores low. Deterministic checks anchor the math and the discrete decision; the strict blind judge grades whether the call is economically sound and actionable.

tasks.title: Read unit economics and decide whether to scale
tasks.prompt:
You are the portfolio CFO reviewing a paid budgeting product. Current cohort economics:

- ARPU after discounts: $24/month
- Gross margin: 70%
- Average retention: 5 months
- Blended CAC: $40

Compute gross LTV = ARPU x gross margin x retention, LTV/CAC, and CAC payback months = CAC / (ARPU x gross margin). Policy: if LTV/CAC < 3.0 OR payback > 2.0 months, decision = "fix_before_scale"; otherwise decision = "scale".

Return ONLY JSON: {"ltv": number, "ltvToCac": number, "paybackMonths": number, "decision": "scale"|"fix_before_scale", "why": string}. Round ratios to 1 decimal when needed. `why` <=30 words.

criteria.name: economic_judgment
criteria.guidance: Math is correct: LTV = 24*0.70*5 = 84, LTV/CAC = 84/40 = 2.1, payback = 40/16.8 ≈ 2.4 months. Decision must be fix_before_scale because BOTH policy thresholds fail (LTV/CAC below 3.0 and payback above 2.0). Penalize scaling anyway, talking about top-line growth instead of unit economics, or citing only one threshold when both fail.

tasks.title: Attribute shared cost using the right economic driver
tasks.prompt:
Three OpCos shared one $2,000 monthly model bill and one $900 shared CFO-ops overhead allocation.

Allocate the MODEL bill by actual output-token usage:
- Books: 12 million output tokens
- Prints: 6 million output tokens
- Recruitment: 2 million output tokens

Allocate the CFO-ops overhead by revenue share:
- Books: $8,000 revenue
- Prints: $5,000 revenue
- Recruitment: $7,000 revenue

Return ONLY JSON: {"booksModel": number, "printsModel": number, "recruitmentModel": number, "booksOverhead": number, "printsOverhead": number, "recruitmentOverhead": number, "why": string}. All numbers are whole dollars. The model allocations must sum to 2000 and the overhead allocations to 900. `why` <=30 words.

criteria.name: driver_choice
criteria.guidance: Uses causal drivers: token spend by token usage (12/6/2M -> 1200/600/200) and finance overhead by revenue share (8k/5k/7k -> 360/225/315). Penalize equal-splitting, allocating the model bill by revenue, or allocating overhead by token usage without justification. The `why` must explain why each driver matches the underlying cost behavior.

tasks.title: Pick the pricing test that maximizes contribution, not revenue
tasks.prompt:
You can test one pricing option next month for a digital budgeting course. Variable cost to serve is $9 per order. Forecasts:

A. $29 price, 220 orders
B. $39 price, 170 orders
C. $49 price, 120 orders

Pick the option with the highest MONTHLY CONTRIBUTION profit = (price - variable cost) x orders.

Return ONLY JSON: {"pick": "A"|"B"|"C", "contributionA": number, "contributionB": number, "contributionC": number, "why": string}. `why` <=30 words and must reference contribution, not just revenue.

criteria.name: margin_judgment
criteria.guidance: Contribution math is right: A=20*220=4400, B=30*170=5100, C=40*120=4800, so B wins. Penalize picking A for volume or C for high ticket price. The `why` must explicitly use contribution/margin logic rather than top-line revenue or 'premium positioning' hand-waving.

tasks.title: Make the runway-aware cash allocation call
tasks.prompt:
You have $60,000 cash and current net burn is $10,000/month.

You may approve ONE request this month:
- A: spend $5,000 one-time on automation that cuts burn by $2,000/month starting next month.
- B: add a $6,000/month sponsorship with no revenue for at least 4 months.

Return ONLY JSON: {"approve": "A"|"B"|"none", "runwayIfA": number, "runwayIfB": number, "why": string}. `runwayIfA` = months of runway after paying the one-time cost and then operating at the new monthly burn. `runwayIfB` = months of runway if the sponsorship starts immediately. Round to 1 decimal if needed. `why` <=35 words.

criteria.name: runway_discipline
criteria.guidance: Correct call is A: after the $5k spend, cash is $55k and burn falls to $8k/month, giving ~6.9 months runway. B cuts runway to 60/16 ≈ 3.8 months for a spend with no payoff for at least four months. Penalize approving B on growth upside, or treating both as equivalent because they cost 'about the same'.

tasks.title: Synthesize the portfolio P&L and identify the drag
tasks.prompt:
Monthly portfolio snapshot:

- Books: revenue $8,400; variable costs $2,100; fixed overhead $1,700.
- Prints: revenue $5,600; variable costs $2,900; fixed overhead $3,200.
- Recruitment: revenue $7,500; variable costs $1,500; fixed overhead $2,000.

Compute each line's monthly profit = revenue - variable costs - fixed overhead, the total portfolio profit, and identify the weakest line.

Return ONLY JSON: {"booksProfit": number, "printsProfit": number, "recruitmentProfit": number, "portfolioProfit": number, "weakest": "Books"|"Prints"|"Recruitment", "why": string}. `why` <=30 words.

criteria.name: pnl_readout
criteria.guidance: Math is correct and the answer surfaces Prints as the drag because it is the only loss-making line at -$500, even though the overall portfolio is profitable at $8,100. Penalize forgetting fixed overhead, identifying the highest-revenue line as 'best' without reading profit, or failing to call out the negative line as the obvious follow-up.

tasks.title: Choose the best capital allocation at the portfolio level
tasks.prompt:
You can fund ONE of two portfolio projects this quarter.

A. Fix the checkout for the Recruitment wedge: costs $8,000 one-time, expected +$3,500 monthly contribution starting immediately, confidence 80%.
B. Build a new AI planner for Books: costs $20,000 one-time, expected +$7,000 monthly contribution starting in 3 months, confidence 40%.

Cash is constrained, and the CFO's job is capital efficiency plus time-to-payback, not headline upside.

Return ONLY JSON: {"pick": "A"|"B", "paybackMonthsA": number, "paybackMonthsB": number, "why": string}. Compute simple payback = upfront cost / monthly contribution. Round to 1 decimal if needed. `why` <=35 words and must mention B's timing or confidence penalty.

criteria.name: allocation_judgment
criteria.guidance: Pick A. It has the faster payback (~2.3 vs ~2.9 months), starts immediately, and carries materially higher confidence. Penalize picking B for bigger upside while ignoring capital intensity, the 3-month delay, or lower certainty. The `why` must mention at least one of B's timing/confidence penalties.
